# Questions in Livestream 192

Live Stream #192: Truths, Facts, and Blessed Facts

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=431" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:11</a> | Is <em>War of the Gods</em> going to be a series? | Yes. He said the second book was already written and the third was partly done. | He answered directly that it is planned as a series. Book one was being shown on stream, book two was already written, and book three was described as roughly three-quarters complete. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:01</a> | Are these books delaying progress on the Ten Plagues book? | No. He said work on the Ten Plagues book was still ongoing, though they still needed a publisher to accept it. | He said the fiction books were not the reason the Ten Plagues project was delayed. The Ten Plagues book was still being pursued, but it had the separate obstacle of needing a publisher to take it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=538" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:58</a> | Is this your science-fiction series? | Yes. He described it as science fiction, roughly a cross between Sherlock Holmes and Indiana Jones in outer space. | He confirmed it was his science-fiction series and framed the premise by genre comparison rather than a detailed plot summary: investigative adventure like Sherlock Holmes and Indiana Jones, but set in outer space. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=623" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:23</a> | Will the book be available in paperback or hardcover? | Both, along with an ebook. He said the hardcover would have a dust jacket. | He said the book would be available in paperback, hardcover, and ebook formats. He added that the ebook had been difficult to format and that sample copies would later let viewers see the hardcover and its dust jacket. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=804" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:24</a> | Are not all relics relics of the dead? | No. He said some relics are associated with divinities or deities, though many medieval European relics were of the dead. | He distinguished medieval Christian-style relics, which often are bodily remains or objects tied to dead saints, from broader religious relics. In other contexts a relic can be connected to a deity or divine object rather than a dead person. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=973" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:13</a> | Would Indiana Jones-style fedoras be good for desert travel? | No. He said they were made more for the American West, are hot in desert conditions, and better desert options exist. | He treated the fedora as a poor desert hat. It was associated more with American West conditions, would be hot on digs, and was less practical than desert headwear designed for sun, heat, and airflow. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=1103" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:23</a> | Why did God make people? | He answered that God creates in order to have relationship with his creation, including humanity. | He answered in relational terms rather than necessity: God did not create because he lacked something, but because creation is ordered toward relationship. Humanity is part of that created relationship with God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=1168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:28</a> | What are your thoughts on the show <em>Expedition Unknown</em>? | He called it a poor show that investigates ancient aliens, paranormal topics, and archaeology in a way he compared unfavorably with <em>Ancient Aliens</em>. | He was sharply critical of the show, grouping its treatment of archaeology with sensational ancient-aliens and paranormal material. His objection was not just style but the way the program presents dubious claims as investigation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=1218" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:18</a> | What is Mr. E's in-universe origin? | He joked that Mr. E spawned from the endless possibilities of "could be." | The answer was intentionally playful rather than lore-heavy. He treated Mr. E as emerging from the recurring "could be" joke, so the origin was comedic and self-referential. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=1345" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:25</a> | How much would it take for you to do a live playthrough of the new Indiana Jones game? | He said video game playthroughs take him away from writing, so they are billed at his normal consulting rate. | He said a game playthrough would consume time he otherwise needs for writing and other work. Because of that opportunity cost, he would treat it like consulting time rather than a normal casual stream request. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=1422" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:42</a> | How much would it take for you to review a book? | He said book reviews are different: they usually do not charge a consulting fee, but the requester needs to send the book, and he was backlogged. | He distinguished book reviews from game playthroughs because a review can benefit more people. He said they normally do not charge a consulting fee for that, but the requester would need to provide the book and wait through an existing backlog. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=1487" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:47</a> | Would watching edited game cutscenes on YouTube work instead of a playthrough? | He said that is not really what they do, and video-game reviews also run into issues such as copyright music and the cost of the game. | He pushed back on replacing a playthrough with a cutscene compilation because it would not match the kind of review they normally do. He also noted practical issues with game coverage, including buying the game and avoiding copyright music. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=1587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:27</a> | Did you get your view of the Nephilim by reading a lot of Bronze Age literature? | Yes. He said repeated exposure to ancient Near Eastern literature led him to see the Nephilim as a priestly caste. | He said the view grew from repeatedly reading ancient Near Eastern texts and noticing patterns in how certain groups function. That cumulative exposure led him to interpret the Nephilim less as a simple biological category and more as a priestly or elite caste. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=1810" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:10</a> | Where did you get your view that true prophets of God do water miracles? | He said ancient Near Eastern literature presents water manipulation as a sign of a prophet with power, and he pointed to biblical examples involving Moses, Joshua, Elijah, and Jesus. | He tied the idea to ancient Near Eastern expectations that powerful prophets could manipulate water. He mentioned Egyptian water magic and biblical examples, using them to explain why Moses, Joshua, Elijah, and Jesus are presented with water-related signs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=1946" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:26</a> | Is water miracle imagery connected to water being chaotic in ancient Egyptian cosmology? | Not necessarily. He said water was viewed as difficult to control, and manipulating large amounts of water carried unusual weight. | He allowed that water can be associated with chaos, but he emphasized control more than cosmological symbolism. The point is that water is hard to command, so moving or restraining large bodies of water marks unusual prophetic power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=2089" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:49</a> | Why are the historical dates you quote often later than the dates found in common reference works? | He said chronology has shifted substantially in recent years and that his dates follow more recent recalibration and computer-generated synchronism work. | He said older common references often preserve older chronological assumptions. His dates follow newer recalibration work, including synchronisms and computer-assisted chronology, so they can differ from widely repeated reference dates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=2531" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:11</a> | Is there a book that integrates biblical history from Terah and Abraham through the conquest with mainstream science, or could you write one? | He recommended Richard Hess's book on ancient Israelite religion as the closest option and said he would like to write such a book if he had time. | He did not know of a single book that fully does what the question asks. He pointed to Richard Hess as a close partial resource and said he would like to write a broader integration himself, but time and writing commitments make that difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=2649" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:09</a> | As vizier, could Joseph have gone back home to see Jacob? | He said a vizier would have had unusual freedom to travel inside Egypt, though he was unsure exactly what "home" meant in the question. | He said a vizier could travel extensively within Egypt as part of the office, including supervising work at places such as tomb sites. He was less certain if the question meant leaving Egypt for Canaan, so he limited the answer to the freedom a vizier would have inside Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=2909" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:29</a> | In Leviticus 10:16-20, what is Aaron's defense and why does it work? | He treated it as a question about the aftermath of Aaron's sons' deaths and explained Aaron's response in that ritual context. | He explained that Aaron's surviving sons had not eaten the sin offering after Nadab and Abihu died. Aaron's defense is that, given the day's deaths and ritual disruption, eating the offering would not have been appropriate, and Moses accepts that contextual explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=3339" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:39</a> | Are Levites Israelites, and how does Numbers 26:64 fit with Eleazar being counted? | He said Levites are Israelites, but they are normally excluded from censuses because they are not counted for ordinary military or tax obligations. | He said Levites are part of Israel, but they occupy a special status as God's unnumbered priests. Because censuses were tied to obligations such as military service, taxes, and corvee labor, Levites were normally excluded even though individual Levites may still appear in narrative or genealogical contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=3546" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:06</a> | Could Levites volunteer for service even though it was prohibited to conscript them? | Yes. He said it was prohibited to conscript Levites, even though some did serve. | He distinguished voluntary service from compulsory conscription. Levites were not supposed to be drafted through the ordinary census system, but that did not mean no Levite ever served in a military or public capacity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=3562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:22</a> | Was the census mainly for counting fighting men, and is that why Levites were excluded? | He said censuses were used for military conscription and corvee labor, and Levites were exempt from those ordinary obligations. | He said ancient censuses commonly served two state purposes: identifying men for war and assigning labor obligations. Since Levites were exempt from those normal obligations, counting them with the rest of the population would not fit the purpose of the census. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=3655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:55</a> | Why does Genesis 10:13-14 list Philistines as descended from Egypt, and is there historical truth to this? | He said Genesis 10 is ethnography rather than a genetic or straightforward historical account, categorizing peoples by cultural association. | He read Genesis 10 as an ethnographic map of peoples and associations rather than a simple biological genealogy. The Philistines can be grouped in relation to Egypt because of cultural and regional associations even if their origin story is more complicated historically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=3907" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:07</a> | What Christmas traditions does the Fulk family have? | He said they keep a fake Christmas tree up year round, partly because no one gets around to taking it down. | He described the family's main tradition as a perpetual artificial Christmas tree. The joke was that the tree stays up in the kitchen year-round, partly from convenience and partly because by the time it might be removed, Christmas is close enough to leave it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=4378" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:58</a> | How outdated is Kenneth Kitchen's book <em>The Bible in Its World</em>? | He said it is dated, but it aged fairly well and contains early forms of Kitchen's core ideas. | He identified it as an older Kitchen work and said the details should be treated as dated. Even so, he thought it aged reasonably well because it already contains themes and arguments that Kitchen developed more fully elsewhere. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=4621" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:01</a> | When did the Hebrew or Jewish belief in reincarnation develop? | He said it appears to be a later Jewish development, probably Common Era or perhaps late Second Temple, but the timing is uncertain. | He was cautious about the date. He said some Jews do believe in reincarnation, but he placed the development later than the Hebrew Bible, probably in the Common Era and perhaps as early as late Second Temple Judaism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=4710" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:30</a> | Why are the bells on the high priest's robe there so he will not die in Exodus 28:34-35? | He said the bells and pomegranates function as hem weights to preserve modesty while the priest ministers. | He argued that the bells should be considered together with the pomegranates around the robe hem. Their practical function was to weigh the garment down so the priest remained properly covered while moving in sacred service. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=4891" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:31</a> | In Exodus 31:17, how is God "refreshed"? | He said the language is not about God being physically rejuvenated, but about ceasing from labor, transcending creation, and admiring the completed work. | He connected the word to rest or taking breath rather than God recovering from exhaustion. The point is that God ceases creative labor and stands over the completed creation, with the language expressing satisfaction and completion rather than physical need. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=5486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:26</a> | Are there publishers that let an author skip a book proposal and just give them the book? | He said a few do, such as Brill, but most publishers require proposals for planning and editorial reasons. | He said some academic publishers may accept a completed book without the same proposal process, naming Brill as an example. Most publishers still want proposals because they need to plan lists, evaluate market fit, and decide editorial commitments before a manuscript is complete. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=5807" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:47</a> | In Exodus 32:32, is Moses asking to die with Israel or in Israel's place? | He said the text is not entirely clear, but Moses is pleading for forgiveness and offering himself as part of that intercession. | He presented Moses' words as intercessory rather than easy to classify as substitution or shared punishment. Moses asks God to forgive Israel and, if not, to blot him out too, so the force is self-offering within a plea for mercy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=5978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:38</a> | Why do early Exodus daters say Rameses has been switched out for Avaris when the Bible says the Israelites built Rameses? | He said they use redaction or later-renaming explanations to preserve the early date, but that creates compounding problems with multiple fixed historical references. | He said early-date arguments often explain Rameses as a later editorial name for Avaris or the same location under a later name. He objected that this creates a chain of special explanations, especially when several chronological references have to be moved or reinterpreted together. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=6395" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:35</a> | What do you mean when you say the Song of the Sea is after the Exodus? | He said parts of the song appear to reflect later stages after the wilderness period and into the conquest, while likely preserving an earlier Mosaic core. | He explained that the song may contain an early core tied to Moses and the sea crossing, but parts of it seem to speak from later in Israel's story. References to peoples and places after the wilderness suggest composition or expansion after subsequent events. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=6787" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:07</a> | What does Exodus 34:14 mean by saying God's name is jealous? | He said "jealous" is better understood as possessive in covenant context: God wants what is his and expects Israel to keep the covenant. | He said modern English makes jealousy sound like a vice, but the covenant sense is closer to rightful possessiveness. God is claiming exclusive covenant loyalty from Israel, like a contract or relationship in which he expects what belongs to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=7083" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:03</a> | Would accepting a historical Exodus and conquest diminish the view that El and Yahweh were originally separate deities? | He said that is probably reasonable, since the Exodus traditions consistently present Yahweh as the actor on Israel's behalf. | He agreed that a more historical Exodus and conquest would make separation of El and Yahweh harder to maintain. In the biblical traditions, Yahweh is the one who acts for Israel in the Exodus, so that history would anchor Yahweh early in Israel's identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=7187" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:47</a> | Where did the Israelites get calendar names such as Abib? | He said Israel seems to have adopted month names from the Canaanite calendar, with the Gezer calendar as an example. | He said Israel's early calendar vocabulary appears to come from the local Canaanite setting. He pointed to evidence such as the Gezer calendar to show that names like Abib fit a broader regional agricultural calendar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=7248" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:48</a> | In Exodus 34:24, does the second "he" refer to Moses or God? | He said he could not identify the grammatical issue in the English or Hebrew and had to pass on the question. | He did not give a substantive answer because he could not see the proposed grammatical problem in either the English or Hebrew text during the stream. Rather than inventing an explanation, he passed on the question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=7413" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:33</a> | What is the significance of Jesus walking on water? | He connected it to water miracle imagery as a sign of a true prophet's power to manipulate water. | He referred back to the earlier water-miracle discussion. Jesus walking on water functions as water mastery, aligning him with the pattern of true prophetic power shown through command over water. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=7452" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:12</a> | Were ear rings or nose rings common in ancient Egypt? | Earrings were very common for men, women, and kings; he said nose rings were exceptionally rare and he could not think of an Egyptian example. | He said earrings were widespread in Egypt across gender and status, including royal examples. Nose rings, by contrast, were so rare that he could not immediately think of an Egyptian example and treated them as exceptional. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=7573" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:13</a> | Were Zoroastrians astrologers? | He said apparently not, based on what he has read from Zoroastrian scholars, though Hittites did practice astrology. | He said his reading of Zoroastrian scholarship does not indicate that Zoroastrians practiced astrology, at least not as a defining feature. He contrasted that with Hittites, whom he said did practice astrology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=7659" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:39</a> | If Hittites are from Anatolia, which is not east of Israel, how does that relate to coming from the east? | He explained that ancient direction language often reflects ethnic geography rather than compass coordinates, so northward travel around the Fertile Crescent could be conceptualized as eastward. | He said ancient geographical labels do not always map to modern compass expectations. Peoples associated with the eastern world could travel by routes that go north around the Fertile Crescent, so Anatolia can still fit the broader directional category in that ancient framing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=7942" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:22</a> | If the Magi came from the east, where were they probably from? | He said they were probably from Turkey, while cautioning that the evidence does not let us know with certainty. | He identified Turkey or Anatolia as the most likely region in his view, connected to the earlier Hittite and eastern-geography discussion. He kept the conclusion tentative because the evidence does not let us pinpoint the Magi's origin with certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=7975" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:55</a> | Did archaeologists exist in ancient Egypt? | He pointed to Khaemwaset as the closest ancient Egyptian example because he investigated, restored, and preserved older monuments. | He said ancient Egypt did not have archaeology as a modern discipline, but Khaemwaset is the closest analogy. Khaemwaset investigated earlier monuments, restored them, and preserved memory of the past in a way that resembles antiquarian activity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=8102" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:02</a> | How does Exodus 6:17 tell us the long lives refer to clans? | He said it does not state it directly, but the demographics require abbreviated clan genealogies rather than only three literal generations. | He said Exodus 6 itself does not explicitly say the long lives are clans, but the surrounding demographic problem forces that reading. The genealogy is too compressed to account for Israel's population if it is only three literal generations, so clan-level genealogy is required. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=8679" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:39</a> | Who is Molech, and why is a ram set aside for it in Leviticus 18:21? | He said Molech is best understood as a kind of sacrifice rather than a god, and he did not see a ram reference in Leviticus 18:21. | He treated Molech as more likely a sacrificial category or rite than the name of a deity. He also said the question's reference to a ram did not match what he saw in Leviticus 18:21, so that part could not be answered as stated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=9015" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:15</a> | Is the story of Cain and Abel an ancient tale of works versus faith? | He rejected that reading as eisegesis and said the story is better read in context as involving pastoralism, agrarianism, and Israel's religious program. | He said reading Cain and Abel as a simple works-versus-faith allegory imports later categories into the text. He preferred to read it within its ancient context, where pastoral and agricultural identities and Israel's religious concerns are more directly relevant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=9259" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:19</a> | Do we equivocate the definition of wisdom in the Old Testament, since Exodus 35:26 seems to denote skill or competency? | He agreed that Hebrew <em>hakma</em> can mean skill, competency, and practical wisdom, such as the ability of a craftsman. | He agreed that wisdom language is broader than abstract moral insight. In contexts like Exodus 35, <em>hakma</em> can refer to skilled ability, technical competence, and practical know-how, especially in craftsmanship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=9397" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:37</a> | Have you read Kim Ryholt's work on the Second Intermediate Period, and how do you assess his seriation chronology? | He said Ryholt gathered useful data, but the book is now badly dated and its conclusions, especially about the Fourteenth Dynasty, are not dependable. | He said Ryholt's collection of material remains useful, but the chronological conclusions have aged poorly. He especially criticized the Fourteenth Dynasty reconstruction, saying later evidence and scholarship make that part unreliable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=10120" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:40</a> | Did you see Wes Huff's appearance on Joe Rogan, and what are your thoughts? | He had not watched it yet, so he could not comment beyond saying it was on his watch list. | He said he had not yet watched the appearance, so he deliberately avoided giving an assessment. The only substantive comment was that it was something he intended to watch. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=10237" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:37</a> | Who is the most underrated pharaoh? | He chose Amenhotep I for restoring stability, reforming government, and setting conditions for later New Kingdom success. | He chose Amenhotep I because he saw him as foundational for later New Kingdom strength. The importance was not flashy conquest but stabilizing the country, reforming administration, and creating conditions that later rulers benefited from. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=10383" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:03</a> | If Egyptian gods were believed to act through their idols, does that correlate with Genesis saying God created man in his image? | Yes. He said the Genesis language uses terms associated with religious images, fitting the idea of humanity as God's image in his temple creation. | He said the comparison is meaningful because image language in the ancient world can refer to cult images through which a deity is represented. Genesis applies that kind of image language to humanity, presenting humans as God's image within his created temple. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=10529" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:29</a> | How much was a talent worth in Matthew's parable? | He said a talent is a large weight of metal rather than a fixed coin value, and in Matthew 25 it represents a very large amount of wealth. | He explained that a talent is a weight measure, so its value depends on the metal and context rather than being a single coin denomination. In the parable, the important point is that it represents a massive amount of entrusted wealth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=10886" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:26</a> | In Hosea 11:5, is it possible to read <em>lo</em> as "surely" instead of "not"? | He said it is possible in some contexts, but in Hosea 11:5 the syntax and historical context favor the normal negative reading. | He acknowledged that <em>lo</em> can be handled differently in some contexts, but he did not think Hosea 11:5 is one of them. The syntax and historical setting make the negative reading stronger than the proposed "surely" reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=11255" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:35</a> | What are your thoughts on Hebrews 11:26-27 mentioning Christ and "the one who is invisible"? | He said Hebrews presents Christ as the fulfillment and payoff of the Old Testament promises, greater than even Moses' vision. | He read Hebrews as interpreting Moses in light of Christ, not as an isolated Old Testament scene. Christ is the fulfillment toward which the promises point, and Hebrews uses Moses' endurance and sight of the invisible one to highlight that greater fulfillment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=11547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:27</a> | How do you interpret Psalm 137:8-9? | He treated the passage as hyperbolic siege language, describing judgment on Babylon in the same terrible terms Babylon had brought on others. | He framed the violent language as ancient siege rhetoric and poetic justice against Babylon. The psalm is not a mild devotional line but a grief-and-judgment text that turns Babylon's own brutality back on Babylon in hyperbolic terms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=12099" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:39</a> | Does the Hebrew word <em>adam</em> mean red in a way that proves Adam's skin color? | No. He said the argument is too simple, that <em>adam</em> means man or humanity, and that Brown-Driver-Briggs is dated for this kind of claim. | He rejected the skin-color argument as an overread of a word association. <em>Adam</em> is used for man or humanity, and he cautioned against leaning on older lexicon claims, especially Brown-Driver-Briggs, to make a biological conclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=12313" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:13</a> | How do we know the date of Khufu, and is it historical or radiometric? | He said Khufu is dated historically through king lists, reign lengths, and related sources, not by radiometric dating. | He said Khufu's date is established through historical chronology rather than primarily through radiocarbon testing. King lists, reign lengths, synchronisms, and related historical controls provide the framework for the date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=12657" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:30:57</a> | Are camels an anachronism in the patriarchal narratives? | No. He said evidence for camel domestication goes back far earlier than Abraham's likely period, so the objection is badly outdated. | He said the older objection depends on an outdated view of camel domestication. Evidence for domestic camels predates the patriarchal setting by enough that camels in those narratives are not automatically anachronistic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=12811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:31</a> | Based on winged goddesses on Egyptian ritual furniture, were the cherubim on the ark female? | Probably. He said the late Bronze Age configuration was likely female winged figures facing each other and forward. | He said the late Bronze Age artistic pattern makes female winged figures plausible for the ark cherubim. His answer was probabilistic, based on comparable ritual furniture and iconography rather than a direct surviving image of the ark. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=12899" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:59</a> | How do throne or sacred-space guardians such as lamassu, cherubim, seraphim, wadjet figures, and sphinxes compare? | He said these are not all equivalent: cherubim and seraphim perform different sacred-space functions, wadjet is a winged solar-serpent motif, and sphinxes symbolize royal power rather than a normal worshiped being. | He warned against flattening all guardian figures into one category. Cherubim and seraphim have distinct roles, wadjet imagery belongs to Egyptian solar-serpent symbolism, lamassu are Mesopotamian protective figures, and sphinxes are tied especially to royal power and sacred space. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=13131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:51</a> | Is it significant that the lifespans from Adam through Moses add up to 12,600 years? | He answered no, saying the Genesis genealogies are complicated by honorific transposition and textual variants, so they should not be treated as a simple arithmetic chain. | He said the proposed total is not significant because the genealogical data are not a straightforward continuous arithmetic timeline. Honorific transposition, abbreviated genealogies, and textual variants make that kind of calculation too unstable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=13408" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:28</a> | How were genealogies tracked in the Old Testament and the ancient Near East? | He said Israelite genealogies often track clan identity with abbreviated relationships, while Egyptian and Mesopotamian practices vary by context. | He explained that genealogies often serve social and clan functions rather than modern biological recordkeeping. Israelite examples can compress generations or identify groups, while Egyptian and Mesopotamian uses differ depending on whether the context is royal, administrative, or family-based. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=13647" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:27</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have poems or stories that did not involve their gods? | He said basically no; Egyptian stories nearly always involve divine elements in some way, much as Greek epics do. | He said Egyptian literature is deeply religious in its assumptions, so divine elements appear almost everywhere. Even stories not narrowly about temple cult still tend to include gods or divine order, much as Greek epic normally involves the gods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=13837" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:37</a> | Do we really know only a tiny fraction of ancient Egypt? | Broadly yes. He said we have the rough skeleton of Egyptian history, but most daily administrative and court-life detail is still missing and most of Egypt remains unexcavated. | He said the broad outlines of Egyptian history are known, but the surviving record is still fragmentary. We lack much of the administrative, court, and ordinary-life detail, and many places or periods remain poorly excavated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=13998" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:18</a> | Do we know much about Roman history? | He said we know more about Roman political history and administration than about Egyptian government because more Roman political writing survived. | He contrasted Roman and Egyptian evidence. Rome preserved more political writing and administrative material, so historians can reconstruct Roman government and elite politics in more detail than many parts of Egyptian administration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_5SSVZ0_soc?t=14051" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:11</a> | Which ages in Abraham's story do you interpret as clans, which as persons, and was Isaac his son or another descendant? | He said the evidence is not clear enough to decide all details, but the very long ages point to clan-level interpretation rather than only one long-lived individual. | He declined to assign every age or relationship with certainty. His main point was that the extremely long ages make more sense at the clan or lineage level, while the text does not give enough evidence to decide every person-versus-clan boundary. |
